name: El Boalo, Soto del Real, Canencia, Lozoya, Navacerrada – circular route
name_original: El Boalo, Soto del Real, Canencia, Lozoya, Navacerrada – ruta circular
region_intro: Road cycling routes around El Boalo are set within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, offering diverse landscapes for cyclists. The region features challenging mountain passes and significant elevation gains, alongside wooded areas, lowlands with ash and holm oak trees, and expansive green meadows. This terrain provides a variety of road cycling experiences, from demanding climbs to routes through natural beauty. Best road cycling routes around El Boalo The most popular road cycling route is El Boalo, Soto del Real, Canencia, Lozoya, Navacerrada – circular route, a 68.8 miles (110.8 km) trail that takes 5 hours 39 minutes to complete. This difficult route traverses multiple mountain passes, including Canencia and Navacerrada. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Manzanares el Real Castle – Santillana Reservoir loop from El Boalo-Cerceda-Mataelpino, a moderate 32.3 miles (51.9 km) path. This route offers views of the Manzanares el Real Castle and the Santillana Reservoir. Local road cyclists also love the Manzanares el Real Castle – Santillana Reservoir loop from El Boalo, a 13.6 miles (21.9 km) trail leading through the local countryside, often completed in about 50 minutes. Road cycling around El Boalo is defined by its mountain passes, wooded landscapes, and views of the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy loops to challenging climbs. The routes in El Boalo are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 40 reviews. More than 6000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore El Boalo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available around El Boalo?
A: There are over 10 road cycling routes around El Boalo, offering diverse experiences within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park. These routes range from easy loops to challenging mountain passes.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in El Boalo?
A: The best time for road cycling in El Boalo is generally from spring through autumn. Summers offer warm, sunny conditions, ideal for exploring the mountain passes. While winter cycling is possible, be aware that higher passes like Navacerrada and Cotos can experience snow and colder temperatures, requiring appropriate gear and caution.

Q: What are the typical terrain and elevation profiles for road cycling in the El Boalo area?
A: Road cycling around El Boalo is characterized by varied terrain, from gentle inclines through wooded landscapes and meadows to significant mountain ascents. You'll encounter challenging passes like Navacerrada, Cotos, Canencia, and Morcuera, which can feature average gradients of 4-6% and occasional sections up to 9%. Routes often involve substantial elevation gains, making it a rewarding area for cyclists seeking a challenge.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling routes in El Boalo?
A: Yes, there are easier routes suitable for families. For instance, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39549008" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Manzanares el Real Castle – Santillana Reservoir loop from El Boalo&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 21.9 km trail with minimal elevation gain, offering a pleasant ride through the local countryside.

Q: What kind of scenic landmarks and viewpoints can I expect along the road cycling routes?
A: The routes offer stunning views of the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park. You can expect to see impressive natural features like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3692534" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peñalara Glacial Cirque&lt;/a&gt;, the peaks of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/121253" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Seven Peaks&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1002532" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Maliciosa Summit&lt;/a&gt;. Routes also pass by historical sites such as the Manzanares el Real Castle and offer panoramic vistas from spots like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/957559" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valdeliganar Viewpoint&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any challenging routes for experienced road cyclists?
A: Absolutely. El Boalo is known for its demanding climbs. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977466269/el-boalo-soto-del-real-canencia-lozoya-navacerrada-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Boalo, Soto del Real, Canencia, Lozoya, Navacerrada – circular route&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 110.8 km trail that traverses multiple mountain passes, including Canencia and Navacerrada, with significant elevation gains. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39564749" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cercedilla train station – Moralzarzal Town Center loop from Mataelpino&lt;/a&gt;, which covers 58 km with nearly 1000 meters of ascent.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about cycling in El Boalo?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 40 reviews. Cyclists frequently praise the diverse landscapes, the challenge of the mountain passes, and the stunning natural beauty of the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park. The variety of routes, from scenic loops to demanding climbs, is also a highlight.

Q: Are there any circular road cycling routes starting from El Boalo?
A: Yes, many routes in the area are designed as circular loops. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977466269/el-boalo-soto-del-real-canencia-lozoya-navacerrada-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Boalo, Soto del Real, Canencia, Lozoya, Navacerrada – circular route&lt;/a&gt;, which offers a comprehensive tour of the region's mountain passes. Another is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31618263" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Manzanares el Real Castle – Santillana Reservoir loop from El Boalo-Cerceda-Mataelpino&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is it possible to access El Boalo by public transport for cyclists?
A: El Boalo and nearby towns like Cercedilla (which has a train station) are generally accessible by public transport from Madrid. However, specific rules regarding bikes on buses or trains can vary, so it's advisable to check with the local transport providers for the most up-to-date information before planning your trip.

Q: Are there specific rules or permits required for cycling within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park?
A: As El Boalo is located within the Sierra de Guadarrama National Park, it's important to be aware of park regulations. While road cycling on designated roads typically doesn't require special permits, it's always recommended to respect park rules, stay on marked routes, and be mindful of wildlife and other park users. For detailed information, consult the official Sierra de Guadarrama National Park website.

Q: Are there any routes that pass by reservoirs or bodies of water?
A: Yes, several routes offer views of local reservoirs. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31618263" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Manzanares el Real Castle – Santillana Reservoir loop from El Boalo-Cerceda-Mataelpino&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/39551048" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Becerril de la Sierra Dam – Navacerrada Reservoir loop from El Boalo-Cerceda-Mataelpino&lt;/a&gt; both feature scenic stretches alongside these water bodies, providing beautiful views and a refreshing atmosphere.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling in El Boalo?
A: El Boalo and the surrounding towns offer various parking options. You can typically find public parking in the village centers, which serve as convenient starting points for many routes. For specific routes, look for designated parking areas near trailheads or popular attractions.

Q: Are there places to eat or stay that are cyclist-friendly in El Boalo?
A: El Boalo and the nearby villages within the Sierra de Guadarrama region are accustomed to outdoor enthusiasts. You'll find a selection of cafes, restaurants, and accommodation options that welcome cyclists. Many establishments offer local cuisine and a chance to refuel after a ride. It's always a good idea to check reviews or call ahead for specific amenities like secure bike storage.
tips:
This is the best preserved castle in the community of Madrid. It is also known as the Mendoza castle because it was this family that built it to reinforce their dominance and influence in 15th-century Castile. Juan Guas, who was the architect of the Catholic Monarchs, participated in its construction.

Its wall testifies to its function as a fortress while its large interior rooms tell of the arrival of new times with less warriors and more palatial and social life. It currently has an interpretation center of the Middle Ages and it carries out outreach activities on this important period in Spain and other cultural activities.
A very nice reservoir. The sunsets and sunrises are spectacular, there are incredible views of Manzanares and its castle, and it is a good place to photograph birds.
Climbing is climbing. Gets steeper as you get closer to Miraflores. Not many cars.
A popular and well-visited spring with clean, sweet water.
It is a huge viaduct located in the heart of the Lozoya valley. One of the most important engineering works in the entire Sierra Norte de Madrid. It has a length of 240 meters.
The viewpoint has incredible views, not only of the reservoir but also of Guadarrama and Peñalara in the background. A quiet place, full of peace, and a good place to rest.

Rascafría is a beautiful village in the heart of the Sierra de Guadarrama mountain range. Located in the Lozoya Valley, it boasts an ideal location for cycling between the Morcuera and Cotos mountain passes.

Its main monument is the Monastery of Santa María del Paular, nestled in a privileged setting blanketed with pine and oak forests. As its name suggests, it is one of the coldest villages in the Madrid region.
Port of passage, with a length of about 18 kilometers, in stage 20 of the Tour of Spain 2015.
